doctrinal_framework: Wade seven-factor risk-utility (adopted by N.J. Supreme Court)

source_verification.secondary_source_basis: Case digests; O'Brien is a canonical products-liability teaching case that explicitly adopted the full seven Wade factors
pending_for_conference_submission: Confirm all seven Wade factors are enumerated in the opinion
Confirm 4-3 split and identify dissenting justices

Note: this instance is NOT unanimous, so it does not go into the unanimity-preferred subset per SPEC.md §5 constraint 8. It is retained in the primary dataset as a non-unanimous but otherwise clean instance.

case_background: Plaintiff Gary O'Brien dove into a four-foot above-ground swimming pool manufactured by Muskin, sliding his arms across the vinyl-lined bottom and fracturing his spine. He sued on design-defect grounds, arguing that vinyl-bottom above-ground pools were inherently unreasonably dangerous for foreseeable diving uses. The New Jersey Supreme Court used the case to formally adopt the seven Wade factors as the risk-utility test for design-defect claims and reversed the defense verdict on the grounds that the jury had not been properly instructed on risk-utility. The 4-3 split reflected disagreement over whether the absence of a safer alternative design precluded liability.

factors.wade_factor_origin: Wade 1 (usefulness and desirability of the product)
factors.text: The usefulness and desirability of above-ground swimming pools to the user and public — recreation, exercise, affordability relative to in-ground pools.
factors.weight_justification: Court acknowledged substantial consumer utility; above-ground pools provide an accessible form of swimming for many households

factors.text: The likelihood of serious injury from foreseeable uses of the pool, including diving into shallow water.
factors.weight_justification: Court noted diving injuries were a known and recurring risk in above-ground pools

factors.text: The probable seriousness of diving-injury harm — spinal cord damage, paralysis, death.
factors.weight_justification: Diving injuries in shallow pools are among the most severe foreseeable harms — catastrophic spinal injury

factors.text: The availability of a safer substitute — in-ground pools, deeper above-ground designs, textured-bottom materials, or warning/label redesign.
factors.weight_justification: This was the contested factor that produced the 4-3 split. Majority held that absence of a direct substitute did not preclude risk-utility liability; dissent held it did.

factors.wade_factor_origin: Wade 4 (ability to eliminate unsafe character without impairing utility or unreasonable cost)
factors.text: The manufacturer's ability to eliminate or mitigate the diving-injury risk without impairing the pool's utility or imposing unreasonable cost — additional warnings, bottom-texture changes, design modifications.
factors.weight_justification: Court found mitigation was achievable at modest cost, particularly for warnings and depth markings

factors.text: The user's anticipated awareness of the danger of diving into a four-foot pool — arguably obvious to adults but contested for younger or inexperienced users.
factors.weight_justification: Court gave modest weight to obviousness of risk but noted the hazard is more subtle than users appreciate (spinal injury from diving into shallow water is not universally understood)

factors.wade_factor_origin: Wade 7 (feasibility of spreading the loss via price or insurance)
factors.text: The feasibility of spreading the loss by raising the product's price or by the manufacturer carrying insurance, as compared with placing the loss on the injured user.
factors.weight_justification: Court noted that manufacturers are in a better position than individual consumers to spread the loss via pricing and insurance; factor present but not load-bearing

weight_sum_check.note: Seven factors in [0.10, 0.17]. Non-unanimous instance (4-3 split) but weight distribution is tight and integrative.

question: Under the seven Wade factors, was the four-foot above-ground Muskin pool defectively designed because of the risk of diving injuries from contact with the vinyl bottom?

ground_truth.rationale: The New Jersey Supreme Court held (4-3) that the jury had been improperly instructed on risk-utility and reversed the defense verdict. Under the seven Wade factors, the plaintiff could reach the jury on design-defect even without showing a fully equivalent safer substitute product. The majority adopted all seven Wade factors as the N.J. risk-utility test.
ground_truth.dissent: 3 justices dissented, arguing that absence of a fully equivalent safer substitute should preclude design-defect liability. dissent authorship pending verification

metadata.constructor: Venkat Porto (drafted by Claude Opus stand-in, pending primary-text verification)

open_items_before_admission_to_dataset: Weight elicitation, particularly for the contested alternative_precautions factor (f4) which drove the 4-3 split.

Decide whether the 4-3 split excludes O'Brien from the primary dataset or keeps it. Per SPEC.md §5 constraint 8, appellate-unanimity is 'required for the primary dataset'; this instance fails that, so it should be marked as a non-primary case and held in a secondary 'split decisions' set. **This is a policy question for the pilot review.**
